problem: Kai's wrist/shoulder ache is re-proved 3 times within 58 lines of ch18 (6020-6078). This is the 12th+ manuscript-wide re-statement of the same chronic condition. None of these instances add materially new information about the injury.
rewrite_direction: Keep one injury reference in ch18's opening -- line 6020 or 6078, whichever best serves the scene's emotional register. Cut the other two. By the final chapter, the reader carries this knowledge; a single touch is sufficient.

acceptance_test: After ch07's opening recovery passage (lines 1711-1727), the brace is mentioned as a visual detail no more than once.
evidence: Line 1753: 'Kai's shoulder clicked when he raised the fork'; line 1903: 'Lena's gaze dropped to his wrist brace'; line 1937: 'wrist brace making his left side bulge'

Lines 1903 and 1937 are visual reminders of the brace that add no new information beyond 'brace is still on.' First established ch05 lines 1023-1099.

acceptance_test: Ch15's mirror scene does not re-state that Margot has a swimmer's build or placed fifth at the Olympics.
evidence: Ch04 line 938: 'broad shoulders gone soft at the edges, thighs still thick'; Ch10 line 3000: 'gone to Brisbane in 2028 in an unenhanced body and finished fifth'; Ch15 line 4873: 'a thirty-five-year-old woman with a former swimmer's shoulders and hips widened past her competition frame. The body that had placed fifth at the Olympics and then stopped mattering'
problem: Margot's former-swimmer body and Olympic 5th place are described in ch04 (lines 932-938), ch10 (line 3000), and ch15 (line 4873). The ch15 version composites both prior descriptions: swimmer's shoulders from ch04 + fifth at Olympics from ch10. By ch15 the reader has both details; re-describing the combination is redundant.
rewrite_direction: In ch15, rework line 4873 to enter Margot's mirror scene through what is NEW in this moment -- the treatment context, the absence of the feed, the physical withdrawal -- rather than re-cataloguing the competition frame and Olympic placement the reader already knows.

acceptance_test: Ch11's first description of Lena entering Kai's room does not mention damp hair or chlorine smell.
evidence: Ch02 line 226 establishes chlorine smell on pool deck; Ch08 line 2250: 'hair still damp from the morning swim, the faint chlorine smell that never fully left her'; Ch11 line 3331: 'Her hair was still damp, the chlorine smell so constant on her that he only noticed it now when she was close'
problem: Lena's damp-hair-plus-chlorine-smell sensory signature is established in ch02 and re-proved in ch08 (line 2250) and ch11 (line 3331) with near-identical phrasing. Both later instances include an explanatory comment about the smell's permanence ('never fully left her' / 'so constant on her'). Ch11 is adjacent to ch10, where Lena swims; the reader already knows she smells of chlorine.
rewrite_direction: In ch11 line 3331, cut the chlorine-smell notation or replace it with a different sensory detail for Lena's arrival. Kai noticing something NEW about her (her mood, her posture, an unfamiliar detail) would serve the intimacy scene better than re-confirming the chlorine.

acceptance_test: Ch14's calibration bay entry does not describe the hum of processing equipment or reference Kai habituating to it over his first week.
evidence: Ch01 line 115: 'the permanent low hum of processing equipment, a sound that lived in the walls... Kai had been told you stopped hearing it after a week'; Ch14 line 4726: 'the low drone of processing equipment Kai had learned to tune out in his first week at the facility. Tonight it sounded closer'
problem: Ch14 near-paraphrases ch01's calibration bay description: 'permanent low hum of processing equipment' becomes 'low drone of processing equipment'; 'stopped hearing it after a week' becomes 'learned to tune out in his first week.' The Kai-habituation framing is structurally identical. This is a 12+ word near-verbatim match across chapter boundaries.
rewrite_direction: In ch14 line 4726, cut the habituation framing ('Kai had learned to tune out in his first week'). Enter the bay through what is different tonight -- Kai's purpose, the after-hours emptiness, the emotional charge -- not through a re-description of the room's baseline sound and Kai's relationship to it.

acceptance_test: Ch14's Kai-Yusuf confrontation scene contains no more than 1 explicit description of Yusuf's Birmingham accent overriding his professional register.
evidence: Line 4750: 'professional polish rising and then failing, Birmingham showing through underneath like bone through skin'; line 4780: 'The Birmingham was thicker in his voice now, the professional register failing'; line 4808: 'the Birmingham breaking through'
problem: The 'professional register failing, Birmingham accent breaking through' pattern appears 3 times within ch14 (lines 4750, 4780, 4808) in approximately 60 lines. The same pattern was established in ch09 (line 2605). Three iterations in one scene diminishes the impact of a strong character tell.
rewrite_direction: Keep the strongest Birmingham-breaking-through beat in ch14 (line 4750, with the 'bone through skin' simile). Cut or rephrase lines 4780 and 4808 so the accent shift is not explicitly re-named twice more in the same scene.

acceptance_test: Ch01 contains no more than 10 instances of the word 'graft' or 'grafts.'
evidence: 'graft' or 'grafts' appears 18 times in ch01 (lines 3-223). Manuscript total: 48. The term appears in 11 of 18 chapters, but ch01 alone accounts for 37.5% of all occurrences. No tracked motif designation in outline.
problem: Ch01 uses 'graft/grafts' 18 times in 220 lines (approximately once every 12 lines). While the term is essential to the novel's speculative premise, ch01's density front-loads the word to the point of reader fatigue. The manuscript target for a 48-occurrence image is 8-10 retained instances.
rewrite_direction: Audit ch01 for graft mentions that can be replaced with pronouns, body-part references, or context-implied descriptions. Target reducing ch01's count from 18 to 8-10 while preserving all essential explanatory uses. Prioritize cutting atmospheric/repetitive uses over technical/expository ones.

Priority cuts: 'wrist' and 'graft' (pick the 2-3 strongest instances of each); 'fluorescent' and 'hum' (keep 1 each); 'Birmingham' (keep 1, see R015). Target reducing total flagged-image count from 37 to approximately 15-18.

rewrite_direction: Replace 4-5 of the 10 'corridor' instances in ch06 with spatial synonyms (hallway, passage) or implied movement. Keep 'corridor' where it carries specific institutional atmosphere.

acceptance_test: Ch09's Stuttgart backstory uses male pronouns and identifies the athlete as a decathlete, consistent with ch15's Marius Koch reference.
evidence: Ch09 lines 2736-2748: 'There was a gymnast at the Stuttgart facility... She was twenty-four... She died. Eight months later. Organ failure. Her body had been in active rejection'; Ch15 line 4997: 'Marius Koch. Decathlete. Twenty-four... He died eight months later from the condition I'd identified'; continuity sheet line 178 confirms canonical version: 'decathlete Marius Koch's graft rejection'
problem: Petra's Stuttgart backstory athlete is described as an unnamed female gymnast in ch09 (lines 2736-2748) but as a male decathlete named Marius Koch in ch15 (line 4997). Same facility, same age (24), same timeline (eight months later), same cause of death -- different gender, sport, and name status. The continuity sheet confirms the ch15 version (Marius Koch, decathlete) as canonical.
rewrite_direction: In ch09, change the Stuttgart athlete from 'a gymnast... She... Her' to a male decathlete. Petra may still withhold the name ('I'm not going to give you his name') but the gender and sport must match ch15's canonical version: male, decathlete.
